title: The Godfather
review: The definitive gangster film. Brando's Vito Corleone is a masterclass in restrained power, and Coppola weaves family loyalty and moral decay into something operatic. Every frame is a painting.

title: Goodfellas
review: Scorsese at his kinetic best. The Copacabana tracking shot alone is worth the price of admission. Ray Liotta narrates the rise and fall of the American gangster dream with an energy that never lets up.

title: Parasite
review: A genre-defying feat. Bong Joon-ho crafts a pitch-black comedy about class that shifts from heist film to horror without missing a beat. The basement reveal is one of the great twists in modern cinema.

title: Dune
review: Villeneuve turns Herbert's dense novel into pure sensory cinema. The scale is breathtaking — Arrakis feels like a real place. Zimmer's score vibrates in your chest. A rare blockbuster that trusts its audience.

title: There Will Be Blood
review: Daniel Day-Lewis devours every scene as Daniel Plainview — a man who builds an empire by draining the earth and the people around him. PTA's magnum opus. The bowling alley scene is burned into my memory.

title: Y Tu Mamá También
review: What starts as a raunchy road trip slowly reveals itself as a meditation on friendship, mortality, and Mexico's class divide. Cuarón's narrator is the secret weapon — quietly devastating.

title: The Seventh Seal
review: Bergman asking the big questions through a chess game with Death. Sparse, haunting, and surprisingly funny in places. The final dance of death on the hillside is iconic for a reason.

title: Interstellar
review: Nolan's most emotional film. The docking scene, the years-lost-in-minutes on Miller's planet, the tesseract — it's a space epic that's really about a father trying to get home. I cried.

title: Whiplash
review: J.K. Simmons is terrifying as Fletcher. The final drum solo is the most exhilarating climax in any film this decade. A brutal, electric exploration of what it costs to be great.

title: The Shawshank Redemption
review: Hope as a survival mechanism, told through one of cinema's great friendships. Freeman's narration is like warm whiskey. The rain scene after Andy's escape — pure cinema catharsis.

title: No Country for Old Men
review: Chigurh is cinema's most terrifying villain — not because of what he does, but because of his philosophy. The Coens strip the Western to its bones and find nihilism underneath. The coin toss scene is flawless.

title: Oppenheimer
review: Nolan makes a three-hour biopic feel like a thriller. Cillian Murphy disappears into the role. The Trinity test sequence is the best thing Nolan has ever directed — sound design that hits you in the chest.

title: City of God
review: A favela epic told with the energy of a bullet. The editing and photography are wild — you can feel the heat and chaos of Rio. Rocket's journey from observer to storyteller is the beating heart of it all.

title: In the Mood for Love
review: The most beautiful film about longing ever made. Wong Kar-wai shows what's NOT said and NOT done, and it hits harder than any love story that gives you the payoff. The slow-motion hallway scenes with that Nat King Cole track — devastating.

title: The Grand Budapest Hotel
review: Wes Anderson's dollhouse aesthetic reaches its peak here. Ralph Fiennes is hilarious and heartbreaking as Gustave. Beneath the whimsy is a surprisingly melancholic story about the death of a world.
